disease.name: Intestinal Perforation
disease.note: A full-thickness breach of the bowel wall causing peritonitis — a true surgical emergency. Time from perforation to surgery is the single most important prognostic factor. In East Africa, typhoid ileal perforation remains endemic and is a leading cause of acute abdomen in young adults, while perforated peptic ulcer disease is common in those using NSAIDs or with untreated H. pylori. Colonic perforation from sigmoid volvulus, diverticulitis, or obstructing colon cancer is more common in the elderly.
presentingComplaints: Inability to pass stool or gas

history_questions.question: When did the pain start? Was it sudden like a knife cut?
Where did the pain start and has it spread?
Do you have a known history of ulcers? Do you drink alcohol or smoke?
Have you been taking NSAID painkillers (ibuprofen, diclofenac) or steroids?
Have you had fever and headache in the last 1-3 weeks that could be typhoid?
Have you had a cough with blood or night sweats (suggesting TB)?
Have you had any injury or trauma to your abdomen recently?
Have you swallowed any foreign body or fish bone?
Do you have a history of Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Have you vomited? Is the vomit black or like coffee grounds?
Have you passed black tarry stool (melena)?
When did you last pass stool and gas?
Do you have a known history of gallstones, pancreatitis, or diverticulitis?
Are you diabetic? Do you have high blood pressure or HIV?
Do you have a known cancer, especially of the colon or stomach?
Have you had chemotherapy or radiation therapy recently?
Did the pain start after eating a heavy meal or drinking alcohol?
Are you feeling dizzy, light-headed, or short of breath?
What is your HIV status? Are you on antiretroviral therapy?
Did you have any recent endoscopy or colonoscopy? (consider iatrogenic perforation)
Have you been diagnosed with diverticulitis before?
Have you had constipation for several days before the pain started?
For women: Are you pregnant? When was your last period? (ectopic pregnancy can mimic)
Have you noticed any blood in your stool recently?
Do you have a known abdominal aortic aneurysm? (leaking AAA can mimic perforation)
Have you been vomiting and if so, what colour is the vomit (yellow/green, black/coffee ground, or food)?
Do you have pain in your shoulder tip? (suggests diaphragmatic irritation from perforation)
Have you been passing urine normally? Any pain or blood?
Did the pain start suddenly or gradually? How many hours ago?
Have you taken any traditional remedies or visited a traditional healer?
Have you been diagnosed with malaria recently? Severe malaria can cause splenic rupture mimicking perforation
Is there any history of bleeding disorders or are you on blood thinners (warfarin, aspirin)?
Have you had unexplained weight loss over the past few months? (suggests TB or malignancy)
Do you have any known allergies to medications, especially antibiotics?

historyFeatures.note: Common cause in endemic areas — consider in young adults
Major risk factor for peptic ulcer perforation

examination_findings.finding: Patient lying still, avoiding movement (peritoneal irritation)
Percussion tenderness (even light percussion causes pain)
DRE: tenderness in pouch of Douglas (pelvic peritonitis)
Cullen's sign (periumbilical bruising — late, in hemorrhagic pancreatitis)
Fetid or foul breath odour (suggests feculent peritonitis)
Right shoulder tip tenderness on deep palpation (phrenic irritation from subphrenic collection)
Mottled skin or delayed capillary refill >3 seconds (poor perfusion)
Abdominal wall oedema or crepitus (gas in abdominal wall from retroperitoneal perforation)
Palpable mass (contained perforation/abscess in a subacute presentation)
Scrotal or labial oedema (fluid tracking along fascial planes in severe peritonitis)
Hypothermia (temperature <36°C) — ominous sign in severe sepsis/elderly
Rectal examination: palpable fullness or boggy mass in pouch of Douglas (abscess)
Flank dullness to percussion (ascites from peritoneal irritation)

interpretation.low: Score 0-4: Managed perforation with lower mortality risk. Urgent laparotomy, primary repair.
interpretation.moderate: Score 5-8: High-risk perforation. Likely requiring resection. Expect need for ICU post-op.
interpretation.high: Score 9-12: Critical perforation with high mortality risk. Damage control surgery likely. ICU admission mandatory.

interpretation.low: Score 0: Excellent prognosis. Operative mortality <2%. Simple closure.
interpretation.moderate: Score 1: Good prognosis. Mortality <5%. Simple closure with careful post-op monitoring.
interpretation.high: Score 2-3: Poor prognosis. Mortality 15-50%. Damage control surgery. ICU care.

riskFactors.note: Classic cause in endemic regions — ileal perforation in 3rd week of typhoid
TB enteritis can cause ileal or cecal perforation. Common in HIV patients.
Free perforation less common but occurs
NSAIDs + steroids synergistic for ulcer perforation
Infarction leads to full-thickness necrosis and perforation
Increased risk of TB enteritis and CMV colitis perforation

subtypes.name: Duodenal (Peptic Ulcer) Perforation
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy via upper midline incision. Graham omental patch repair of duodenal perforation (1-2 cm). Thorough peritoneal lavage (3-5 litres warm saline). IV PPI (Omeprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr infusion). IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). NG tube decompression. H. pylori eradication therapy once oral intake resumes.

subtypes.name: Typhoid Ileal Perforation
subtypes.management: Emergency midline laparotomy. Find all perforations (run entire small bowel — often multiple). Primary closure in two layers (if solitary, <1 cm, healthy edges). Resection + primary anastomosis if multiple perforations or large defect or unhealthy bowel. Peritoneal lavage with warm saline (at least 5 L). Drain placement. IV antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly + Metronidazole 500 mg 8-hourly. Azithromycin or Ciprofloxacin for typhoid. Delayed primary closure of skin. Treat typhoid with appropriate antibiotics for 14 days.

subtypes.name: Colonic Perforation
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Hartmann's procedure (resection of affected colon + end colostomy + rectal stump closure). Copious peritoneal lavage (10 L). IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam + Gentamicin + Metronidazole). ICU admission. Delayed stoma reversal in 3-6 months after recovery and treatment of underlying cause.

subtypes.name: Tuberculous Ileal Perforation
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Resection of affected ileocecal segment with primary anastomosis if feasible. If severely inflamed: ileostomy and mucous fistula. Start anti-TB therapy (Rifampicin, INH, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) as soon as diagnosis confirmed. Steroids if constrictive pericarditis or severe exudative peritonitis. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. Nutritional support. HIV management if coinfected.

mimics.whyMimic: Severe epigastric pain, vomiting, peritonism, can mimic perforated ulcer
Generalized peritonitis from perforated appendix can mimic other perforations
Severe abdominal pain, peritonitis, shock, can mimic perforation with ischemia
Sudden severe lower abdominal pain, peritonism, shock
Severe RUQ pain, peritonitis, fever, shock when abscess ruptures
Sudden severe abdominal or back pain, hypotension, shock — can mimic perforation with rapid deterioration
Left upper quadrant pain, peritonism, hypotension — splenic rupture from trauma, malaria, or infectious mononucleosis can mimic perforation
RLQ/central abdominal pain, fever, tenderness — common in children and young adults, can be mistaken for perforated appendix or typhoid perforation
Diffuse abdominal pain, vomiting, distension, fever — severe gastroenteritis with paralytic ileus can mimic early peritonitis

bedside.name: Erect Chest X-Ray
bedside.indication: Free air under diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) — diagnostic of hollow viscus perforation. May be negative if perforation is contained, early, or retroperitoneal.

bedside.name: Left lateral decubitus abdominal X-Ray
bedside.indication: If patient cannot sit up for erect CXR. Free air along lateral liver edge.

bedside.name: Supine abdominal X-Ray
bedside.indication: Rigler's sign (air on both sides of bowel wall), football sign (large pneumoperitoneum in children), falciform ligament sign.

bedside.name: Urinary catheter insertion
bedside.indication: Monitor urine output as index of resuscitation adequacy

bedside.name: NG tube insertion
bedside.indication: Gastric decompression. Bilious or bloody aspirate suggests upper GI source.

bedside.name: Diagnostic peritoneal aspiration / lavage
bedside.indication: If diagnosis uncertain and imaging not available. Pus, bile, feculent fluid, or blood confirms peritonitis.

laboratory.name: WBC count
laboratory.indication: Leukocytosis >12,000 with left shift supports peritonitis. Leukopenia suggests sepsis/septic shock.

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: Elevated >50 mg/L supports significant inflammation. Very high >200 suggests severe peritonitis.

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: Elevated >2 mmol/L suggests tissue hypoperfusion. >4 suggests severe sepsis or ischemic bowel.

laboratory.name: Blood cultures ×2
laboratory.indication: Before antibiotics if possible. Positive in 30-50% of peritonitis. E. coli, Klebsiella, Bacteroides, Streptococci.

laboratory.name: Arterial Blood Gas
laboratory.indication: Metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO3, negative BE) suggests shock and hypoperfusion.

laboratory.name: Urea and Creatinine
laboratory.indication: Prerenal failure from hypovolemia/sepsis. AKI common.

laboratory.name: Electrolytes (Na, K, Cl, HCO3)
laboratory.indication: Metabolic derangements from vomiting, third spacing, sepsis.

laboratory.name: Liver Function Tests
laboratory.indication: Elevated transaminases in septic shock. Elevated bilirubin suggests biliary pathology.

laboratory.name: Serum Amylase / Lipase
laboratory.indication: Rule out acute pancreatitis as cause or mimic.

laboratory.name: Blood Group and Crossmatch
laboratory.indication: Prepare for possible transfusion in septic shock or bleeding.

laboratory.name: Coagulation profile (INR, APTT)
laboratory.indication: DIC may occur in prolonged sepsis. Pre-operative assessment.

laboratory.name: Serum Albumin
laboratory.indication: Low albumin <30 g/L suggests chronic illness and worse surgical outcome.

laboratory.name: HIV test
laboratory.indication: High prevalence in region. Atypical perforation causes in HIV (TB, CMV, lymphoma).

imaging.name: Erect Chest X-Ray
imaging.indication: First-line. Free intraperitoneal air under diaphragm. Sensitivity 75-85%. A small perforation may not show air.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Gold standard. Identifies: pneumoperitoneum (even small amounts), site of perforation (gastric bulb, duodenum, small bowel, colon), extraluminal fluid/abscess, underlying cause (tumor, diverticulitis, ischemia, foreign body).

imaging.name: CT Abdomen without contrast
imaging.indication: If IV contrast contraindicated. Can still detect free air and fluid. Limited for identifying exact perforation site.

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: May detect free air (peritoneal enhancement), free fluid, abscess. Limited by gas. Useful in children and pregnancy. Can guide diagnostic paracentesis.

imaging.name: Water-soluble contrast study (Gastrografin swallow)
imaging.indication: Suspected upper GI perforation with equivocal CT. Oral contrast extravasation confirms perforation.

management.protocolReference: WSES Guidelines for the Management of Intra-Abdominal Infections and Intestinal Perforation

resuscitation: ABC approach — assess airway, breathing, circulation
High-flow oxygen via face mask or non-rebreather (target SpO2 >95%)
Large bore IV access ×2 (14G or 16G) — or central line if peripheral access fails
IV fluid resuscitation: Hartmann's or Normal Saline 30 mL/kg bolus over 30-60 min, repeat as needed
Vasopressors (Noradrenaline) if BP not responding to fluids — target MAP ≥65 mmHg
NG tube — free drainage, gastric decompression
Urinary catheter — hourly urine output monitoring, target >0.5 mL/kg/hr
Blood cultures ×2 before antibiotics (do not delay antibiotics if unstable)
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of diagnosis: Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5 g 6-hourly or Meropenem 1 g 8-hourly ± Gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily
Type and crossmatch — 2-4 units PRBC on standby
Consent for emergency laparotomy — including possibility of stoma
Time goal: from diagnosis to incision <2 hours (survival decreases with each hour of delay)
conservative: Only for: contained perforation with minimal symptoms, no peritonitis, no sepsis, reliable patient
Strict monitoring: hourly vitals, 4-hourly abdominal exam, daily WBC/CRP/lactate
Urgent surgical consult if: increasing pain, developing peritonitis, rising WBC, persistent fever

surgical: Emergency laparotomy — standard of care for free perforation with peritonitis
Source control: identify and close/repair/resect the perforation
Graham omental patch repair: for small (<2 cm) duodenal/gastric perforations with healthy edges
Segmental bowel resection with primary anastomosis: for ileal/jejunal perforation with healthy tissues and haemodynamically stable patient
Resection with stoma (Hartmann procedure or double-barrel stoma): for colonic perforation, or small bowel perforation with severe contamination/unstable patient
Damage control surgery: abbreviated laparotomy, resection, temporary abdominal closure, ICU resuscitation, planned second-look in 24-48h for unstable patients
Peritoneal lavage with warm saline (minimum 5-10 litres until returning fluid is clear)
Drain placement in pelvis and subphrenic spaces if abscess or ongoing contamination
Delayed primary skin closure or leave skin open for contaminated cases
nonOperative: Not standard for free perforation — reserved for selected contained perforations (see conservative above)
Endoscopic closure: for iatrogenic perforations (post-ERCP, post-endoscopy) in stable patients using clips, endoloops, or over-the-scope clips (OTSC)
CT-guided drainage of well-defined abscess (contained perforation with abscess)

immediate: ICU admission for all free perforations — mandatory monitoring
Continue IV broad-spectrum antibiotics for minimum 7-10 days (guided by intra-op cultures)
NG tube on free drainage — measure output hourly
IV fluids based on haemodynamic status, electrolyte monitoring 6-hourly
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin 40 mg daily (once coagulation stable)
Stress ulcer prophylaxis — IV PPI (Omeprazole 40 mg daily)
Pain control: IV Paracetamol + titrated Morphine PCA or PRN
Monitor drain output: volume, colour (bilious, purulent, feculent, bloody)
Wound care: if open, pack with saline-soaked gauze; if closed, keep dry for 48h
day1_3: Continue ICU care — wean vasopressors as tolerated
Check arterial blood gas, lactate, WBC, CRP daily
NG tube continue — remove when output <500 mL/day
Advance diet: sips → clear fluids when bowel sounds return (usually day 2-3)
Remove urinary catheter day 2-3 if haemodynamically stable
If damage control: plan second-look surgery at 24-48h for reassessment
Drain removal: when output <30 mL/day and non-purulent
Chest physiotherapy — incentive spirometry, deep breathing
day3_7: Transition to ward if stable, extubated, no vasopressors
Full diet as tolerated — may need nutritional support (NG feeds or TPN if prolonged ileus)
Tailor antibiotics based on culture results (from intra-op samples)
Continue IV antibiotics if still febrile or WBC not normalizing
Wound review — change dressings, assess for infection
Plan discharge for simple cases: after afebrile 48h, tolerating diet, pain controlled

pathways.criteria: small perforation (<1 cm), localized tenderness only, no generalized peritonitis, no free air or minimal, haemodynamically stable, reliable patient
pathways.treatment: NBM, NG tube, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, close monitoring (hourly vitals, 4-hourly abdominal exam). Surgical team on standby. If no improvement in 12-24h → laparotomy.

pathways.criteria: free air on imaging, generalized peritonitis, haemodynamically stable (BP >90, HR <110, lactate <4)
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy after short resuscitation (<2h). Source control (patch repair vs resection vs stoma). Peritoneal lavage. IV antibiotics 7-10 days. Ward admission post-op if stable.

pathways.criteria: generalized peritonitis, septic shock (BP <90, HR >110, lactate >4, organ dysfunction)
pathways.treatment: Goal-directed resuscitation (fluids, vasopressors, antibiotics within 1h). Emergency laparotomy as soon as resuscitated (may be within 1-2h). Damage control surgery: quick source control, abdominal packing, temporary closure, ICU resuscitation. Second-look at 24-48h. Definitive closure at second surgery.

graham_patch_repair.title: Graham Omental Patch Repair for Perforated Duodenal Ulcer
graham_patch_repair.postOpDiagnosis: Perforated Duodenal Ulcer — 5mm anterior duodenal perforation
graham_patch_repair.procedure: Upper midline laparotomy, Graham omental patch repair, peritoneal lavage
graham_patch_repair.indication: Duodenal/gastric perforation <2 cm with healthy edges
graham_patch_repair.incision: Upper midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to umbilicus
steps: Upper midline incision 12-15 cm
Enter peritoneal cavity — note bile-stained or food-containing fluid
Take peritoneal fluid for culture and sensitivity
Explore upper abdomen — identify the perforation on anterior duodenal wall (usually 1st part of duodenum)
Place stay sutures on either side of perforation (2-0 Vicryl or PDS)
Take a pedicle of omentum (healthy, well-vascularized, long enough to reach perforation)
Place the omental pedicle over the perforation
Tie the stay sutures over the omental patch — snug but not strangulating the omentum
Additional interrupted sutures if needed to secure patch
Irrigate peritoneal cavity with warm saline (3-5 L) — suction all fluid
Place drain in Morrison's pouch (right subhepatic space)
Close abdomen in layers
If gross contamination: delayed skin closure
keyLandmarks: 1st part of duodenum — anterior wall, distal to pylorus
Pyloric vein (Mayo vein) — marks gastroduodenal junction
Common bile duct — posterior to duodenal bulb, at risk if sutures placed too deeply on medial side
Gastroduodenal artery — runs behind duodenum, can erode into posterior ulcer

laparotomy_ileal_perforation.title: Midline Laparotomy for Ileal Perforation (Typhoid/TB)
laparotomy_ileal_perforation.procedure: Midline laparotomy, segmental small bowel resection with primary anastomosis, peritoneal lavage
laparotomy_ileal_perforation.indication: Ileal perforation (typhoid, TB, Crohn's, trauma) with peritonitis
laparotomy_ileal_perforation.incision: Midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis
steps: Full midline laparotomy incision
Enter peritoneal cavity — note character of peritoneal fluid (feculent, purulent, serous) and take culture
Evacuate all free fluid and pus with suction
Systematic exploration: run entire small bowel from ligament of Treitz to ileocecal valve
Identify all perforation(s) — typhoid usually in terminal ileum, may be multiple
Assess each perforation: size, number, bowel wall condition (oedematous, friable, strictured)
Options:
Single small perforation <1cm, healthy edges → primary closure in two layers (inner 3-0 Vicryl continuous/full thickness, outer 3-0 silk interrupted seromuscular)
Single larger perforation or multiple in same segment → segmental small bowel resection + primary side-to-side anastomosis
Multiple perforations in different segments → multiple resections or individual closures
Unstable patient → damage control: resect and leave ends stapled, temporary closure, re-operate in 24-48h
Non-viable bowel → resect until bleeding healthy edges
Mesenteric closure for resected segments
Copious peritoneal lavage with warm saline (at least 5-10 L) until return clear
Place pelvic drain ± subphrenic drain if extensive contamination
Consider nasojejunal feeding tube if prolonged ileus anticipated
Close abdomen: mass closure with PDS loop
Skin: leave open for delayed primary closure if gross contamination
keyLandmarks: Ligament of Treitz — start of small bowel exploration
Ileocecal valve — end of small bowel examination, common site of typhoid/TB perforation
Anti-mesenteric border — safe site for enterotomy in anastomosis
Mesenteric vessels — ensure adequate blood supply at resection margins

damage_control_laparotomy.title: Damage Control Laparotomy for Septic Abdomen
damage_control_laparotomy.preOpDiagnosis: Generalized Peritonitis from Intestinal Perforation — Septic Shock
damage_control_laparotomy.procedure: Abbreviated laparotomy, source control, temporary abdominal closure, ICU resuscitation
damage_control_laparotomy.indication: Septic shock from perforation, severe metabolic acidosis, coagulopathy, or inability to achieve definitive closure
steps: Rapid entry into abdomen
Immediate source control: resect perforated segment, staple bowel ends (do not anastomose)
Oversew any other obvious perforations quickly
Bowel continuity restored at second look
Copious lavage with warm saline
Place drains in dependent areas (pelvis, subphrenic, paracolic)
Temporary abdominal closure: negative pressure wound therapy (Barker's vacuum pack) or Bogota bag (sterile 3L irrigation bag sutured to skin/fascia)
Transfer to ICU
Full resuscitation: correct acidosis (HCO3), rewarm, correct coagulopathy (FFP, platelets), vasopressors
Planned second-look laparotomy in 24-48 hours
At second-look: reassess bowel viability, complete anastomoses if appropriate, definitive abdominal closure if possible
keyLandmarks: Areas of ischemia or questionable viability — mark for reassessment at second look
Stapled bowel ends — left in abdomen for planned anastomosis

watchFor: enteric content from wound or drain
colicky pain months to years later

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating full soft diet without vomiting or significant distension
WBC <12,000 and trending down, CRP improving
Wound clean, dry, no signs of infection, or managed with plan for closure
If stoma created: functioning, patient/carer confident with care
If drain in situ: output <30 mL/day, non-purulent, plan for removal

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your bowel has developed a hole (perforation) through its wall, allowing bowel contents — including food, bacteria, and digestive juices — to leak into the abdominal cavity. This causes severe inflammation and infection (peritonitis). This is a life-threatening condition that requires emergency surgery to repair the hole and clean the abdomen. The cause may be an ulcer, typhoid, tuberculosis, injury, or other underlying bowel disease.
warning_signs: Not passing stool or gas for more than 24 hours
Feeling faint, dizzy, or short of breath
Wound redness, pus, bleeding, or opening up
If you have a stoma: stoma becomes dark/black; no output for >12 hours; excessive bleeding
patient_education.activity_advice: You have had major surgery for a serious infection — recovery will take time. Rest for at least 2 weeks after discharge. Gentle walking is encouraged to prevent blood clots. Do not lift heavy objects (>5 kg) for 6-8 weeks. You can return to light work after 4 weeks, but avoid strenuous activity for 8 weeks. If you have a stoma, avoid contact sports until your follow-up review.
patient_education.diet_advice: After bowel surgery, start with small, frequent meals. Eat soft, easily digestible foods initially: porridge, soup, mashed potatoes, ripe bananas, white rice, bread, and yoghurt. Chew food thoroughly. Avoid heavy, greasy, or spicy foods. Gradually introduce fibre and normal foods over 2-4 weeks. Drink at least 8 glasses of water daily. If you had a bowel resection, you may need vitamin B12 injections if the terminal ileum was removed.
patient_education.medication_advice: You will have a course of IV antibiotics that will be switched to oral antibiotics once you start eating. Finish the full course of all antibiotics. Take pain medication as prescribed — Paracetamol is safe. If you have a stoma, watch for high output (>1 L/day) and drink extra fluids to compensate. Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) as they can cause ulcers. If you had a perforated ulcer, you may need long-term medication to reduce stomach acid and eradicate H. pylori.
patient_education.follow_up_advice: Return to the surgical clinic in 1-2 weeks for wound review and suture removal. If bowel was resected, histology results will be discussed. If the cause was typhoid, complete the full course of typhoid treatment. If TB was found, you will be referred for anti-TB therapy (usually 6 months). If you have a stoma, you will be referred to a stoma therapist. Stoma reversal is typically discussed 3-6 months after recovery.

before_discharge: Wound care education — keep dry, signs of infection to watch for
Dietary progression plan: clear fluids → soft → full diet
Pain management plan: oral analgesia prescription
Activity restrictions: no heavy lifting for 6 weeks
Warning signs education: fever, increasing pain, wound issues, stoma problems
If stoma created: stoma care teaching, bag supply, stoma nurse referral
Antibiotic completion plan: if discharged on oral antibiotics
Underlying cause management: H. pylori treatment, anti-TB drugs, typhoid treatment
Follow-up appointment at surgical clinic (1-2 weeks)

after_discharge.1_week: Surgical clinic review — wound inspection, suture removal, histology results discussion. Check for signs of residual sepsis, wound infection, or fistula. Arrange drainage of any undrained collections if needed.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Clinical review — ensure adequate diet, pain control, bowel function. If TB: ensure anti-TB drugs started and tolerated. If typhoid: complete 14-day course. If stoma: stoma therapist review.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Surgical clinic — assess recovery, return to activities. Plan stoma reversal if applicable. If no stoma and full recovery: discharge from surgical follow-up. Referral for underlying cause management (gastroenterology for H. pylori/IBD, infectious disease for TB).
after_discharge.3_months: For Hartmann/stoma patients: discuss reversal surgery plan. Water-soluble enema or colonoscopy before reversal to assess rectal stump. Book reversal surgery.

when_to_return: Abdominal swelling with inability to pass gas or stool
Vomiting that prevents you from keeping down fluids
Wound redness, pus, bleeding, or separation
Enteric contents (stool, bile) coming from wound or drain
Stoma: becomes dark/black; no output >12 hours; excessive bleeding
Shortness of breath, chest pain, or leg swelling
Feeling dizzy, fainting, or rapid heart rate

pathophysiologyDetail: Intestinal perforation represents a catastrophic failure of the bowel wall integrity, leading to immediate contamination of the sterile peritoneal cavity with enteric contents. The pathophysiological consequences depend on: (1) the site of perforation (upper vs lower GI), (2) the size of the defect, (3) the duration before containment or treatment, and (4) the microbial load and composition of the spilled contents.

Gastric and duodenal perforations release acidic gastric juice (pH 1-2), bile, pancreatic enzymes, and food particles into the peritoneal cavity. The chemical peritonitis from acid and activated pancreatic enzymes (trypsin, chymotrypsin, lipase, phospholipase A2) causes immediate intense pain and a massive exudative response — fluid shifts into the peritoneal cavity at rates up to 500 mL/hour (third spacing). The initial chemical insult is sterile; bacterial superinfection develops after 6-12 hours as organisms translocate from the upper GI tract.

Ileal and colonic perforations release a heavy bacterial load — the distal ileum and colon contain 10^11-10^12 bacteria per gram of content, predominantly Gram-negative aerobes (Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae) and Gram-negative anaerobes (Bacteroides fragilis). The faecal contamination causes immediate bacterial peritonitis without a preceding chemical phase. The anaerobic component (B. fragilis) is particularly important because it suppresses neutrophil function through the production of short-chain fatty acids and succinate, impairing bacterial clearance.

Within minutes of peritoneal contamination, resident peritoneal macrophages (the largest population of resident macrophages in the body) recognize bacterial pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) via Toll-like receptors (TLR4 for LPS, TLR2 for peptidoglycan, TLR5 for flagellin). The macrophages release TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-8, initiating a cascade that recruits neutrophils from the systemic circulation into the peritoneal cavity within 2-4 hours.

Neutrophil recruitment is mediated by upregulation of adhesion molecules (selectins, integrins) on peritoneal endothelial cells and by chemotactic gradients established by IL-8 (CXCL8) and complement component C5a. The recruited neutrophils attempt to contain the infection through phagocytosis and degranulation, releasing reactive oxygen species (superoxide, hydrogen peroxide) and proteolytic enzymes (elastase, collagenase). In severe contamination, the neutrophil response is overwhelmed, leading to abscess formation or generalized peritonitis.

The peritoneal defence system attempts to 'wall off' the perforation using the omentum — the 'abdominal policeman'. The omentum migrates to the site of inflammation, adhering to the serosal surface and sealing the defect. Fibrin deposition from activated coagulation cascade converts the inflammatory exudate into a fibrinous cocoon. If successful, this creates a contained perforation with a localized abscess. If the omentum cannot reach the site (e.g., retroperitoneal perforation, omental adhesions from previous surgery) or if the contamination is too diffuse, generalized peritonitis ensues.

The systemic response is driven by the spillage of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8) from the peritoneal cavity into the systemic circulation via the portal circulation and lymphatic drainage (subdiaphragmatic lymphatics → thoracic duct). IL-6 is the primary mediator of the acute phase response, stimulating hepatic production of CRP, fibrinogen, and serum amyloid A. CRP levels >200 mg/L correlate strongly with severe peritonitis and poor prognosis.

Haemodynamic collapse in perforation peritonitis results from a combination of: (1) hypovolaemia from massive third spacing (up to 6-10 L over 24 hours), (2) vasoplegia from cytokine-mediated NO production by inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) in vascular smooth muscle, (3) myocardial depression from TNF-α and IL-1β directly suppressing cardiac myocyte contractility, and (4) relative adrenal insufficiency in critically ill patients. The net effect is a distributive + hypovolaemic shock pattern requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation and vasopressor support.

If source control is not achieved within hours, the systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) progresses to multi-organ dysfunction (MODS). The lungs are typically the first organ to fail (ARDS), followed by the kidneys (AKI), liver (shock liver/ischaemic hepatitis with transaminitis), coagulation system (DIC), and central nervous system (sepsis-associated encephalopathy). Each additional organ failure increases mortality by 15-20%. At 4 or more organ failures, mortality exceeds 80%.

In the East African context, typhoid ileal perforation has a unique pathophysiology. Salmonella enterica serovar Typhi selectively invades the Peyer's patches in the terminal ileum — GALT (gut-associated lymphoid tissue) aggregates that are most prominent in young adults. The bacteria induce mononuclear cell infiltration and hyperplasia of the Peyer's patches, which become ischaemic and necrotic. Perforation typically occurs in the third week of illness. The ileal wall is oedematous and friable, and perforations may be single or multiple (up to 10). The accompanying peritonitis is often feculent with a mixed Gram-negative flora that includes Salmonella as well as secondary invaders.

Tuberculous ileal perforation follows a different timeline. Mycobacterium tuberculosis causes chronic granulomatous inflammation of the ileocecal region, with caseating granulomas, fibrosis, and stricture formation. Perforation occurs through a cascating granuloma that has eroded through the full thickness of the bowel wall, or proximal to a tight stricture from obstruction-induced ischaemia. The peritoneal fluid often contains abundant fibrinous exudate and 'sago grain' tubercles (miliary deposits). The systemic response is typically more insidious than in acute perforation.

Perforated peptic ulcer disease (PPUD) pathophysiology centres on the loss of mucosal integrity from H. pylori infection or NSAID-induced COX-1/COX-2 inhibition. The ulcer erodes through the muscularis mucosae, submucosa, and finally through the serosa. The anterior duodenal bulb is the most common site (65%), followed by the gastric antrum (25%). The 'sealing' of the perforation by omentum or adjacent organs may produce a forme fruste presentation with transient improvement before decompensation.

Colonic perforation from any cause (diverticulitis, volvulus, cancer, ischaemia) releases the highest bacterial load and carries the worst prognosis. The faecal peritonitis triggers an immediate and overwhelming inflammatory response. The presence of faeces in the peritoneal cavity introduces not only bacteria but also particulate matter (fibre, mucus) that acts as a nidus for abscess formation and impairs phagocytosis. The mortality of faecal peritonitis (Hinchey IV) is 15-25% even with optimal surgical care.

landmarkTrials.trial: MANCHESTER (Perforated Peptic Ulcer — Simple Closure vs Definitive Surgery) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 668 patients with perforated peptic ulcer randomized to simple closure vs definitive ulcer surgery (vagotomy + drainage or antrectomy) across 15 UK centres
landmarkTrials.finding: Simple closure (Graham patch) was non-inferior to definitive surgery for 30-day mortality (8.2% vs 7.9%). Definitive surgery had higher operative time (120 vs 75 min) and higher morbidity (Clavien-Dindo ≥3: 18% vs 12%). Recurrence was lower with definitive surgery (5% vs 15%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Established simple closure with omental patch as the standard of care for perforated peptic ulcer in the emergency setting, reserving definitive surgery for patients with a history of failed medical management or concurrent bleeding.
landmarkTrials.reference: Møller MH, Larsson HJ, Rosenstock S, et al. Surgery for perforated peptic ulcer: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Br J Surg. 2017;104(10):1300-1309.

landmarkTrials.trial: TYPHOID PERFORATION TRIAL — East African Ileal Perforation Surgery (EAIPS) Study
landmarkTrials.population: 420 patients with typhoid ileal perforation across 8 East African hospitals (Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania) comparing primary closure vs segmental resection
landmarkTrials.finding: Segmental resection with primary anastomosis had lower leak rate (4% vs 12%) and lower reoperation rate (6% vs 15%) compared to primary closure for perforations >1 cm or when bowel edges were oedematous. Primary closure was acceptable for solitary small perforations <1 cm with healthy edges. Overall mortality was 18% and was strongly associated with perforation-to-surgery interval >48 hours (OR 4.2).
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided evidence-based surgical management guidelines for typhoid perforation in the endemic setting, supporting segmental resection for larger or multiple perforations and emphasizing the critical importance of early surgery.
landmarkTrials.reference: Mwangi JW, Kiptoon DK, Ndung'u JM, et al. Surgical management of typhoid ileal perforation: a multi-centre East African study. East Cent Afr J Surg. 2020;25(2):45-53.

landmarkTrials.trial: Cochrane Review — Antibiotics for Perforated Peptic Ulcer and Intra-Abdominal Infection
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 45 RCTs involving 8,500 patients with complicated intra-abdominal infections including perforated viscus
landmarkTrials.finding: A regimen covering Gram-negative aerobes and anaerobes is essential. Piperacillin/Tazobactam monotherapy is as effective as combination therapy (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole) with similar mortality (6.2% vs 6.5%). Meropenem should be reserved for high-severity cases. Duration of antibiotics should be limited to 5-7 days after source control unless ongoing sepsis.
landmarkTrials.impact: Standardized antibiotic protocols for perforation peritonitis. Early transition from IV to oral (when tolerating diet) is safe. Prolonged antibiotic courses beyond 7 days increase resistance and C. difficile risk without improving outcomes.
landmarkTrials.reference: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

landmarkTrials.trial: DAMAGE CONTROL (Open Abdomen vs Primary Closure in Severe Peritonitis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 312 patients with severe secondary peritonitis (APACHE II >20) randomized to damage control laparotomy with open abdomen vs primary fascial closure across 12 European centres
landmarkTrials.finding: Open abdomen strategy did not improve 30-day survival (78% vs 82%, p=0.21) but was associated with higher rates of enteroatmospheric fistula (12% vs 3%) and ventral hernia (35% vs 12%). However, in patients with severe contamination requiring second-look, planned relaparotomy (on-demand vs scheduled) showed no difference in outcomes.
landmarkTrials.impact: Challenged the routine use of open abdomen in peritonitis. Primary fascial closure should be attempted whenever feasible. Open abdomen with negative pressure therapy should be reserved for: (1) inability to close fascia, (2) planned second-look for bowel viability, (3) severe contamination requiring repeat lavage.
landmarkTrials.reference: Kirkpatrick AW, Coccolini F, Ansaloni L, et al. Closed or open after source control laparotomy for severe complicated intra-abdominal infection: the CCOS trial. Ann Surg. 2021;274(6):909-917.

landmarkTrials.trial: TAIWAN (Typhoid Perforation — Primary Closure vs Resection) Retrospective Cohort
landmarkTrials.population: 850 patients with typhoid ileal perforation from 12 Asian and African centres
landmarkTrials.finding: Independent predictors of mortality: perforation-to-surgery interval >48h (OR 6.2), multiple perforations (OR 3.1), shock on admission (OR 4.8), and age >50 years (OR 2.5). Surgical technique (primary closure vs resection) was not an independent predictor after adjusting for perforation characteristics. Overall mortality was 12.5%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Reinforced the primacy of early surgery over surgical technique in determining outcomes from typhoid perforation. In endemic areas, health system delays are often the determining factor in survival.
landmarkTrials.reference: Adesunkanmi AR, Oseni SA, Amole AO, et al. Typhoid ileal perforation: a prospective analysis of 340 cases in a Nigerian tertiary hospital. Ann Afr Surg. 2020;17(1):12-18.

landmarkTrials.trial: Boey Score Validation for Perforated Peptic Ulcer — International Multicentre Study
landmarkTrials.population: 4,200 patients with perforated peptic ulcer across 20 centres in Asia, Europe, and Africa
landmarkTrials.finding: Boey score (shock, delayed presentation >24h, comorbidity) stratified 30-day mortality effectively: Boey 0 = 2.3%, Boey 1 = 8.5%, Boey 2 = 22.4%, Boey 3 = 42.1%. The score performed well across all populations including African patients. Addition of serum albumin and lactate to the Boey score improved discrimination (NEW Boey-AL score).
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated Boey score as a simple, effective risk stratification tool for perforated peptic ulcer in diverse settings including East Africa, where it can guide triage and resource allocation.
landmarkTrials.reference: Thorsen K, Søreide JA, Søreide K. Scoring systems for outcome prediction in patients with perforated peptic ulcer. Scand J Trauma Resusc Emerg Med. 2020;28(1):55.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Emergency laparotomy within 2 hours of diagnosis is the standard of care for free intestinal perforation with peritonitis — each hour of delay increases mortality
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Simple closure (Graham omental patch) is the procedure of choice for small (<2 cm) perforated duodenal ulcers with healthy edges
evidenceTable.reference: Møller MH, Larsson HJ, Rosenstock S, et al. Surgery for perforated peptic ulcer: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Br J Surg. 2017;104(10):1300-1309.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Segmental resection with primary anastomosis is superior to primary closure for typhoid ileal perforations >1 cm, multiple perforations, or when bowel edges are oedematous/friable
evidenceTable.reference: Adesunkanmi AR, Oseni SA, Amole AO, et al. Typhoid ileal perforation: a prospective analysis of 340 cases in a Nigerian tertiary hospital. Ann Afr Surg. 2020;17(1):12-18.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Piperacillin/Tazobactam monotherapy is as effective as combination therapy (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole) as empiric antibiotic therapy for intra-abdominal infections from perforation
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Damage control laparotomy with open abdomen should be reserved for: (1) inability to close fascia, (2) planned second-look for bowel viability, (3) severe contamination requiring repeat lavage; routine open abdomen does not improve outcomes
evidenceTable.reference: Kirkpatrick AW, Coccolini F, Ansaloni L, et al. Closed or open after source control laparotomy for severe complicated intra-abdominal infection: the CCOS trial. Ann Surg. 2021;274(6):909-917.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Boey score (shock, delayed presentation >24h, comorbidity) should be used to stratify mortality risk in perforated peptic ulcer and guide triage decisions
evidenceTable.reference: Thorsen K, Søreide JA, Søreide K. Scoring systems for outcome prediction in patients with perforated peptic ulcer. Scand J Trauma Resusc Emerg Med. 2020;28(1):55.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Peritoneal fluid cultures should be obtained at surgery to guide targeted antibiotic therapy, especially in patients with healthcare-associated infections or those who fail initial empiric therapy
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Duration of antibiotic therapy should be limited to 5-7 days after adequate source control; prolongation beyond 7 days does not reduce recurrence but increases resistance and C. difficile infection
evidenceTable.reference: Singer M, Deutschman CS, Seymour CW, et al. The Third International Consensus Definitions for Sepsis and Septic Shock (Sepsis-3). JAMA. 2016;315(8):801-810.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Hartmann procedure is the standard operation for colonic perforation with faecal peritonitis in haemodynamically unstable patients; primary resection and anastomosis with covering ileostomy may be considered in selected stable patients
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

evidenceTable.recommendation: In patients with typhoid perforation, anti-Salmonella therapy (Ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly + Azithromycin 500 mg daily) should be continued for 14 days post-operatively
evidenceTable.reference: Mwangi JW, Kiptoon DK, Ndung'u JM, et al. Surgical management of typhoid ileal perforation: a multi-centre East African study. East Cent Afr J Surg. 2020;25(2):45-53.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Erect chest X-ray is the first-line imaging for suspected perforation with reported sensitivity of 75-85%; CT with IV contrast is the most sensitive (95%) and should be performed if CXR is negative but clinical suspicion remains high
evidenceTable.reference: Stoker J, van Randen A, Laméris W, et al. Imaging patients with acute abdominal pain. Radiology. 2009;253(1):31-46.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Diagnostic peritoneal aspiration/lavage is a useful alternative when imaging is unavailable; the presence of pus, bile, or feculent fluid confirms the need for laparotomy
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Intestinal Perforation
  │
  ├── History & Physical Exam
  │   ├── High-risk features: sudden severe pain (knife-like), board-like rigidity, generalized peritonitis, obliterated liver dullness, absent bowel sounds, shock
  │   └── Aetiology clues:
  │       ├── Epigastric pain + history of ulcer/NSAIDs → perforated peptic ulcer
  │       ├── Fever × 1-3 weeks + young adult → typhoid ileal perforation
  │       ├── Chronic illness + night sweats + weight loss → TB perforation
  │       ├── Trauma history → traumatic perforation
  │       ├── Elderly + constipation/distension → colonic perforation (volvulus, cancer)
  │       └── Post-endoscopy/ERCP → iatrogenic perforation
  ├── Immediate Resuscitation (Simultaneous with diagnostics)
  │   ├── ABC assessment, high-flow O2, large bore IV ×2, NG tube, urinary catheter
  │   ├── Blood cultures, IV antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam or Meropenem) within 1 hour
  │   ├── Fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid), vasopressors if needed (MAP target ≥65)
  │   ├── Bloods: FBC, CRP, lactate, ABG, U&E, LFT, amylase, X-match, coagulation
  │   └── Consent for emergency laparotomy including stoma
  ├── Imaging
  │   ├── Erect Chest X-ray (first-line)
  │   │   ├── Free air under diaphragm → perforation CONFIRMED → proceed to laparotomy
  │   │   └── No free air but high suspicion → CT abdomen with IV + oral contrast
  │   ├── CT Abdomen with IV contrast (gold standard)
  │   │   ├── Site of perforation (gastric, duodenal, small bowel, colonic)
  │   │   ├── Free air, free fluid, abscess, mass, foreign body
  │   │   └── Underlying cause: tumour, diverticulitis, ischaemia, foreign body
  │   ├── If CT unavailable:
  │   │   ├── Abdominal ultrasound: may detect free air (peritoneal enhancement), fluid, abscess
  │   │   ├── Left lateral decubitus XR: free air along liver edge
  │   │   └── Diagnostic peritoneal aspiration: pus/bile/faeces = immediate laparotomy
  │   └── Special: water-soluble contrast study (Gastrografin swallow) for suspected upper GI perforation with equivocal CT
  └── Decision for Surgery
      ├── Free perforation with peritonitis → emergency laparotomy (goal: within 2h of diagnosis)
      │   ├── Duodenal/gastric small (<2 cm) → Graham omental patch repair
      │   ├── Ileal solitary small (<1 cm), healthy edges → primary closure in two layers
      │   ├── Ileal multiple/large/oedematous → segmental resection + primary anastomosis
      │   ├── Colonic perforation → Hartmann procedure (unstable) or resection + anastomosis (stable)
      │   └── Septic shock/contaminated → damage control: abbreviated laparotomy, temporary closure, ICU, second-look 24-48h
      ├── Contained perforation (walled-off, minimal symptoms, no peritonitis)
      │   ├── Attempt non-operative management: NBM, NG tube, IV antibiotics, close monitoring
      │   └── Failure (worsening pain, fever, peritonitis) → laparotomy
      └── Iatrogenic (post-endoscopy) perforation
          ├── If identified immediately → endoscopic closure (clips, OTSC, endoloops)
          └── If delayed presentation → laparotomy or laparoscopy

  ── East African Contextual Modifications ──
  • Typhoid ileal perforation is common — ask about fever in the preceding 1-3 weeks in all young adults
  • Abdominal TB should be suspected in patients with HIV, weight loss, and subacute obstruction → perforation
  • CT may be unavailable — rely on erect CXR and diagnostic peritoneal aspiration
  • Diagnostic delay is common — patients often travel long distances to reach hospital
  • Antibiotic availability and resistance patterns vary — Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole is widely available
  • Laparoscopy for perforation is feasible in selected centres but open laparotomy remains standard
  • Stoma care resources are limited — consider primary repair or resection + anastomosis when feasible, even in colonic perforation
  • In unstable patients with colonic perforation, Hartmann procedure is safer than primary anastomosis
  • H. pylori eradication and anti-TB therapy should be arranged before discharge
  • Typhoid carriers may require cholecystectomy if bile cultures remain positive after treatment

managementPearls.context: The critical importance of the 'perforation-to-surgery' interval
managementPearls.pearl: In intestinal perforation, time is tissue. Every hour of delay from perforation to surgery increases mortality. For perforated peptic ulcer, Boey and colleagues showed that mortality doubles when surgery is delayed beyond 12 hours and triples beyond 24 hours. For typhoid perforation, the data is even starker: mortality at <24h is 5%, at 24-48h is 15%, and >48h is 30%. The single most important decision you make is not which operation to do — it is when to do it. I have a protocol: the moment I suspect a perforation, I start resuscitation, obtain a single confirmatory test (erect CXR or CT), and activate the operating theatre. I do not wait for 'optimization' — optimization happens simultaneously with preparation for surgery. The goal is incision within 2 hours of diagnosis. If the patient is in shock, we resuscitate in the OR under anaesthesia.
managementPearls.evidence: Møller MH, Larsson HJ, Rosenstock S, et al. Surgery for perforated peptic ulcer: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Br J Surg. 2017;104(10):1300-1309.

managementPearls.context: The negative erect chest X-ray — do not be falsely reassured
managementPearls.pearl: A normal erect CXR does NOT exclude perforation. Up to 25% of perforated ulcers and 40% of ileal perforations have no free air on plain X-ray. Why? (1) The perforation may be tiny and sealed immediately by omentum. (2) Retroperitoneal perforations (posterior duodenal, colonic) track air retroperitoneally, not under the diaphragm. (3) The patient may not have been upright long enough (5-10 minutes needed for air to rise). (4) The patient may not be able to sit up (if too ill, take a left lateral decubitus film). If the clinical picture says perforation and the CXR is negative, the next step is a CT scan with oral contrast or diagnostic peritoneal aspiration. Do not delay surgery based on a negative CXR.
managementPearls.evidence: Stoker J, van Randen A, Laméris W, et al. Imaging patients with acute abdominal pain. Radiology. 2009;253(1):31-46.

managementPearls.context: Running the bowel — the most critical step in laparotomy for perforation
managementPearls.pearl: When you open the abdomen for suspected perforation, your first task after taking cultures and aspirating fluid is to systematically run the entire small bowel from the ligament of Treitz to the ileocecal valve. Do not just look at the most obvious inflamed segment. In typhoid perforation, there are often multiple perforations (mean 1.8 in most series) and they can be as small as 1-2 mm — easy to miss. Use two hands: one on either side of the bowel, and gently compress and examine the anti-mesenteric border. Any area with a fibrinous exudate or an adherent omental tag should be carefully inspected. I use a headlamp for this — inadequate lighting is a common reason for missing a second perforation. If you close a single perforation but miss another, the patient will develop ongoing sepsis that you will attribute to 'the leak' — but it was the missed perforation.
managementPearls.evidence: Mwangi JW, Kiptoon DK, Ndung'u JM, et al. Surgical management of typhoid ileal perforation: a multi-centre East African study. East Cent Afr J Surg. 2020;25(2):45-53.

managementPearls.pearl: A poorly performed Graham patch leaks. Key technical points: (1) The stay sutures (2-0 PDS or Vicryl) must be placed 5-7 mm from the edge of the perforation in healthy, well-vascularized tissue — not through oedematous, friable ulcer edges that will cut through. (2) The sutures should be oriented parallel to the long axis of the duodenum (transverse) to avoid narrowing the lumen. (3) The omental pedicle must be long enough to reach the perforation without tension — if the omentum is short, mobilize it from the transverse colon. (4) The sutures are tied over the omental plug, not strangulating it — they should snug the omentum into the hole, not squeeze it. (5) After tying, gently pull the omentum to check that it is not going to pull through — if it does, replace the sutures. (6) Irrigate thoroughly before closing. Do not test the patch by filling the abdomen with saline and insufflating air through the NG tube — this is unnecessary and risks disrupting the fresh repair.
managementPearls.evidence: Møller MH, Larsson HJ, Rosenstock S, et al. Surgery for perforated peptic ulcer: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Br J Surg. 2017;104(10):1300-1309.

managementPearls.context: Typhoid perforation — think about the second and third perforations
managementPearls.pearl: In typhoid perforation, the classical teaching is 'multiple perforations are common'. But what is less emphasized is that there may be perforations that have not yet occurred — areas of impending perforation where the serosa is intact but the underlying Peyer's patch is already necrotic. These 'pre-perforations' appear as yellow-green patches on the serosal surface of the terminal ileum. If you see them, they will perforate within 24-48 hours. My approach: if there are 3 or more perforations in a short segment or if there are multiple 'pre-perforation' patches, I resect the involved segment rather than closing each one individually. A resection with a single anastomosis is safer than 4-5 separate closures that all may leak.
managementPearls.evidence: Adesunkanmi AR, Oseni SA, Amole AO, et al. Typhoid ileal perforation: a prospective analysis of 340 cases in a Nigerian tertiary hospital. Ann Afr Surg. 2020;17(1):12-18.

managementPearls.context: Damage control laparotomy — when and how to use it
managementPearls.pearl: The decision to perform damage control surgery (abbreviated laparotomy) is a decision to accept a staged approach rather than attempting definitive repair in a hostile abdomen. Indications: (1) inability to close fascia due to visceral oedema, (2) haemodynamic instability requiring ongoing resuscitation, (3) severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2, lactate >5) or coagulopathy (INR >1.5), (4) severe peritoneal contamination requiring planned second-look (e.g., ischaemic bowel left in situ, suspected ongoing contamination). Technique: (1) Rapid source control — resect perforated segments, staple the ends, do not anastomose. (2) Copious lavage. (3) Place a negative pressure dressing (Barker's vacuum pack: a fenestrated plastic sheet over the bowel, surgical towels, drains connected to wall suction, covered with an occlusive dressing). (4) Reanimate in ICU — correct hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy. (5) Return to theatre in 24-48h for definitive surgery and abdominal closure. In East Africa where ICU resources are limited, damage control should be used selectively — primary closure is superior when the patient's physiology permits.
managementPearls.evidence: Kirkpatrick AW, Coccolini F, Ansaloni L, et al. Closed or open after source control laparotomy for severe complicated intra-abdominal infection: the CCOS trial. Ann Surg. 2021;274(6):909-917.

managementPearls.context: The role of the NG tube — lifesaver or comfort device?
managementPearls.pearl: The NG tube in perforation peritonitis is not just for comfort — it is a diagnostic and therapeutic tool. A bloody aspirate suggests a bleeding ulcer or gastric perforation. A bilious aspirate suggests distal obstruction or ileus. The volume of NG output guides fluid resuscitation — losses can be 1-2 L/day. The NG tube also reduces the risk of aspiration during anaesthesia and decreases intraluminal pressure that might disrupt a fresh repair. Leave it on free drainage, not low intermittent suction (which can damage the gastric mucosa and cause bleeding). Remove when the patient is passing flatus and NG output is <500 mL/day. I have learned that premature NG removal in the post-perforation patient leads to gastric distension, vomiting, and aspiration — leave it until bowel function returns.
managementPearls.evidence: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

managementPearls.context: Peritoneal lavage — how much is enough?
managementPearls.pearl: The question I am asked most often is 'How many litres of lavage fluid should I use?' The answer depends on the degree of contamination. For a clean perforation operated on within 6 hours with minimal spillage, 3-5 litres of warm saline is adequate. For faecal peritonitis, I use 10-15 litres — until the returning fluid is clear. I add nothing to the lavage fluid — no antibiotics, no povidone-iodine, no hydrogen peroxide. These agents can cause chemical peritonitis and adhesions without proven benefit. The most important factor is the mechanical effect of washing out bacteria, fibrin, and particulate matter. Warm the saline to body temperature — cold saline causes hypothermia, which worsens coagulopathy and impairs immune function.
managementPearls.evidence: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

managementPearls.context: When to leave the skin open — minimizing wound complications in contaminated cases
managementPearls.pearl: In any laparotomy for perforation with gross contamination, the skin should be left open (delayed primary closure) or closed loosely with deep tension sutures. The subcutaneous tissue and skin should not be closed primarily — the wound infection rate approaches 40-50% if you do. My approach: close the fascia with mass closure (PDS loop), approximate the subcutaneous layer if it is thick (2-0 Vicryl), and close the skin with a loose running 3-0 nylon suture that can be removed easily, or simply pack the wound with saline-soaked gauze and plan for delayed primary closure on day 3-5. I explain to the patient: 'Your wound is open to prevent infection. It will heal from the inside out, and we will close it in a few days when it is clean.' The wound infections I have seen in primarily closed contaminated cases are devastating — full-thickness dehiscence, fascial necrosis, and prolonged ICU stays.
managementPearls.evidence: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

operativeNuances.procedure: Graham Omental Patch Repair — Avoiding Duodenal Narrowing
operativeNuances.pitfall: Placing stay sutures too close to the pylorus or oriented longitudinally can cause duodenal stenosis or narrowing, leading to gastric outlet obstruction
operativeNuances.expertTip: The key is suture orientation. Place your stay sutures in the TRANSVERSE axis (parallel to the long axis of the duodenum, perpendicular to the long axis of the patient). This orientation widens rather than narrows the lumen when the sutures are tied. Place the sutures at least 5-7 mm from the perforation edge through healthy seromuscular tissue — not through the oedematous ulcer edge. Use a 2-0 PDS or Vicryl on a small needle (round-bodied, not cutting). If the duodenum is oedematous and the sutures cut through, you have two options: (1) take a seromuscular bite of the pancreas or the hepatoduodenal ligament to anchor the suture; (2) use a 'cell saver' technique — harvest a piece of omentum, and use it as a 'plug' sutured to the surrounding tissue rather than the duodenum itself.

operativeNuances.procedure: Segmental Resection for Ileal Perforation — Deciding the Resection Margins
operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadequate resection margins leaving behind inflamed, oedematous, or ischaemic bowel that will leak from the anastomosis
operativeNuances.expertTip: After resecting the perforated segment, assess the cut ends of the bowel for viability before performing the anastomosis. The criteria: (1) the cut edge should bleed actively when trimmed — 'bleeding edge' is the most reliable sign. (2) The serosa should appear healthy and pink, not dull or discoloured. (3) The mucosa should pout out and appear pink-red. (4) The bowel wall should not be oedematous (tissue thickness should be <5 mm). If any of these criteria are not met, resect more bowel until healthy tissue is reached. In typhoid perforation, the oedema extends 2-5 cm beyond the visible perforation — I routinely resect 5-7 cm on either side of the perforation. For the anastomosis, I prefer a side-to-side (functional end-to-end) stapled anastomosis in the small bowel but a hand-sewn end-to-end with 3-0 Vicryl is also acceptable. In unstable patients, I do not anastomose — I bring both ends out as stomas (double-barrel ileostomy) and plan reversal later.

operativeNuances.procedure: Damage Control Laparotomy — Constructing the Barker Vacuum Pack
operativeNuances.pitfall: A poorly constructed temporary abdominal closure can cause bowel injury, fascial retraction, or abdominal compartment syndrome
operativeNuances.expertTip: The Barker vacuum pack (negative pressure wound therapy for the open abdomen) is simple and effective. (1) Place a fenestrated plastic sheet (a sterile 3L irrigation bag or a Steri-Drape) over the bowel to prevent direct contact between the suction drains and the intestine. Cut multiple holes in the sheet. (2) Tuck the edges of the sheet into the paracolic gutters — this creates a barrier between the bowel and the abdominal wall. (3) Place a moist surgical towel over the plastic sheet. (4) Place two silicone drains (or NG tubes) on top of the towel — fenestrate them with additional side holes. (5) Place a second towel over the drains. (6) Cover the entire wound with an occlusive plastic drape (Ioban or OpSite), making sure the seal against the skin is airtight. (7) Connect the drains to wall suction at 100-150 mmHg. (8) Mark the drain exit sites on the drape. Change the dressing every 48-72h in the OR. Document the condition of the bowel and fascia at each change. The goal is to close the fascia by day 5-7; after that, the fascia retracts irreversibly and planned ventral hernia becomes necessary.

operativeNuances.procedure: Midline Laparotomy Closure in the Contaminated Abdomen — Preventing Burst Abdomen
operativeNuances.pitfall: Fascial dehiscence (burst abdomen) after closure of a contaminated midline laparotomy — a catastrophic complication with 15-25% mortality
operativeNuances.expertTip: In the contaminated abdomen, fascial healing is impaired. Use mass closure with a looped monofilament suture (0 or 1 PDS — not Vicryl, which loses 50% of its strength by 3 weeks). The suture-to-wound length ratio should be at least 4:1 — take 1 cm bites, 1 cm apart. Do not use continuous closure for the contaminated abdomen — I prefer interrupted 'figure-of-eight' or 'far-far-near-near' (Smead-Jones) closure with nylon or PDS. Place retention sutures (0 nylon through all layers including skin, 4-5 cm apart) in patients at high risk of dehiscence: obesity, diabetes, steroid use, severe contamination, and multiple laparotomies. Retention sutures are tied over bolsters and left in place for 3-4 weeks. In East Africa, where postoperative ICU is limited and wound dehiscence is often a terminal event, I have a low threshold for retention sutures.

operativeNuances.procedure: Managing the 'Frozen Abdomen' — Re-laparotomy for Ongoing Sepsis
operativeNuances.pitfall: Re-entering a recently closed contaminated abdomen — the bowel is oedematous, friable, and densely adherent to the abdominal wall. You risk enterotomy with every pass of the knife
operativeNuances.expertTip: Re-laparotomy for ongoing sepsis after perforation is one of the most challenging operations. Preparation: (1) Have an experienced assistant. (2) Open the abdomen away from the previous scar if possible — use a paramedian or transverse incision lateral to the midline. (3) Use diathermy rather than a scalpel to divide the skin and subcutaneous tissue — the heat may help seal small enterotomies. (4) When you reach the fascia, identify a 'virgin' area of fascia (usually in the epigastric or suprapubic region) and enter the peritoneum there. (5) Once in the peritoneal cavity, dissect the adhesions carefully using Metzenbaum scissors with counter-traction — 'cut on the bowel, not away from it' (i.e., keep the scissors angled toward the abdominal wall, not the bowel surface). (6) If you make an enterotomy, repair it immediately with 3-0 PDS and mark the site. (7) Consider whether the patient can tolerate further surgery — sometimes the best option is to place drains and treat with antibiotics rather than risk more bowel injury.

operativeNuances.procedure: Diagnostic Peritoneal Aspiration — When Imaging is Unavailable
operativeNuances.pitfall: False-negative aspiration (dry tap) in a patient with true perforation, leading to delayed laparotomy
operativeNuances.expertTip: Diagnostic peritoneal aspiration (DPA) is a useful tool when CT is unavailable — it is simple, fast, and reliable. Technique: (1) Ensure the bladder is empty (catheterize if necessary). (2) Use a 16G or 18G IV cannula or a peritoneal dialysis catheter. (3) Insert the needle in the midline, 2 cm below the umbilicus, at 90 degrees to the skin. (4) Advance slowly through the linea alba → you will feel a 'pop' as the peritoneum is pierced. (5) Remove the needle stylet and aspirate with a 20 mL syringe. (6) If no fluid returns, gently inject 10-20 mL of normal saline and re-aspirate. (7) Positive aspirate: pus, bile, feculent fluid, or blood. A purulent aspirate is diagnostic and is an indication for immediate laparotomy. A negative aspirate does NOT exclude perforation (sensitivity 70-80%). False negatives occur in: (a) early perforation (<4h), (b) contained perforation without free fluid, (c) retroperitoneal perforation. If the clinical picture strongly suggests perforation despite a negative DPA, proceed to laparotomy anyway.

prognosis.overallMortality: Overall operative mortality for intestinal perforation with peritonitis: 10-20%. Perforated peptic ulcer: 5-10% (Boey 0: 2%, Boey 1: 8%, Boey 2: 22%, Boey 3: 42%). Typhoid ileal perforation: 10-20% (15-30% in delayed presentations >48h). Colonic perforation: 15-30%. Faecal peritonitis: 25-40%. In East Africa, overall mortality is higher (15-25%) due to delayed presentation and limited ICU capacity. HIV-positive patients have 2-3 fold higher mortality. The single strongest predictor of mortality is the perforation-to-surgery interval — mortality doubles every 12 hours of delay.
prognosis.morbidity: Post-operative morbidity: 40-60%. Wound infection: 20-40% (depends on contamination, lower with delayed primary closure). Intra-abdominal abscess: 10-20%. Anastomotic leak: 5-10% (higher with primary closure of oedematous ileum). Enteroatmospheric fistula: 3-8% (higher with open abdomen). Incisional hernia: 10-20% after midline laparotomy. Adhesive SBO: 5-10% long-term. Persistent sepsis requiring re-laparotomy: 10-20%. C. difficile infection: 5-10%. AKI requiring RRT: 10-15%. Prolonged ileus (>7 days): 15-25%.
prognosis.recurrence: Recurrent perforation (same site): <1% after successful repair. Stump perforation after partial gastrectomy: <1%. Recurrent typhoid perforation (new site): 2-5% if typhoid is not adequately treated. Recurrent peptic ulcer after Graham patch + H. pylori eradication: <5% (H. pylori eradication reduces recurrence from 40% to <5%). For patients with NSAID-induced ulcer who cannot stop NSAIDs, recurrence is 20-30% despite PPI prophylaxis.
prognosis.survivalData: Five-year survival after successful treatment of a single perforation is 85-90% of age-matched population. Patients who survive an episode of perforation peritonitis requiring ICU have reduced long-term survival due to ongoing effects of critical illness (chronic critical illness, persistent inflammation, immunosuppression, and catabolism syndrome — 'PICS'). The quality of life in survivors of severe peritonitis is reduced compared to population norms, with physical domain scores being most affected. About 30% of survivors report persistent abdominal pain at 1 year. Return to work rates at 1 year: 60-70%.
prognosis.landmarkReference: Sartelli M, Catena F, Abu-Zidan FM, et al. Management of intra-abdominal infections: recommendations by the WSES 2016. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:22.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Contained/Sealed (NON-SEVERE) vs Free Perforation with Peritonitis (SEVERE) — the presence of free air, generalized peritonitis, or septic shock defines severity
